LITERAL.description: Answer explicitly stated in one sentence, no transformation required

PARAPHRASE.description: Same idea as passage in different wording, conceptual mapping required

INFERENCE.description: Requires combining 2+ passage parts or performing intermediate logical/math step

DEEP_INFERENCE.description: Must actively disprove wrong answers using restrictive qualifiers, multiple plausible options remain after first pass

AMBIGUITY_RESOLUTION.description: 2+ options seem correct, distinction depends entirely on qualifiers/scope/certainty level

transition_conditions.Inference_to_DeepInference: >=2 options remain plausible after first-pass elimination

HARD_blueprint.description: Hard RC questions combine inference requirement with maximum answer choice similarity. Two options survive first-pass elimination and are distinguishable only by a single qualifier or scope difference. The passage is typically abstract or philosophical with high noun density.
HARD_blueprint.answer_engineering: 2 options both textually supported at surface level, distinguished only by certainty/scope/qualifier. Correct option uses weak qualifier (may, suggests). Wrong option uses strong qualifier (must, necessarily, always).
HARD_blueprint.generation_note: Hard RC = Medium RC problem + answer choices engineered to maximum similarity. Do NOT increase passage complexity — increase option indistinguishability.

TEXTUAL_ECHO.mechanism: Copies exact words from passage but scrambles syntactic relationship

EXTREME_LANGUAGE.mechanism: Takes qualified passage statement and upgrades to absolute

SCOPE_SHIFT.mechanism: Applies valid claim to broader/narrower/different scope

OUTSIDE_KNOWLEDGE.mechanism: Factually true in real world but absent from passage

HALF_TRUE.mechanism: First clause matches passage, second clause contradicts

NUMERICAL_CLOSENESS.mechanism: Requires exact mathematical deduction from passage data

error_frequency_map.most_common: Pattern-matching instead of semantic comprehension — Textual Echo Traps
error_frequency_map.second_most_common: Failing to verify exact scope of modifiers — Extreme Language Traps
error_frequency_map.third_most_common: Accepting outside knowledge as passage evidence — Outside Knowledge Traps

high_cost_question_types: Negative factual questions requiring verification of 4 true statements

quantifiable_signals.shared_opening: >=3 options with same opening phrase forces deep reading
quantifiable_signals.abstract_noun_density: High usage of system/structure/relation slows conceptual mapping

layer1_stem_transformation.rule: Replace direct interrogatives with global evaluation stems
layer1_stem_transformation.examples: Change 'When did X occur?' to 'Based on the passage, which statement is true regarding X?'
observed_signals: 'Based on the information... it is correct to state that'

layer2_cognitive_load.rule1: Add extra step: require mathematical subtraction of two stated numbers (Mock1Q7)
layer2_cognitive_load.rule2: Invert search parameter to negative logic: 'Which statement is false/not in line'

layer3_answer_engineering.similarity_injection: Begin multiple options with identical phrasing to prevent skimming
layer3_answer_engineering.target_model: 1 Correct (Paraphrase) + 1 Textual Echo + 1 Extreme Language + 1 Outside Knowledge + 1 Scope Shift

layer4_ambiguity_injection.mechanism: Introduce >=2 options both logically plausible and text-adjacent
layer4_ambiguity_injection.execution: Distinguish only by qualifiers, scope, or certainty level

line_based.description: Isolates single phrase, traps use real-world definition instead of contextual

paragraph_based.description: Tests relationships within segment, traps invert cause/effect

global.description: Requires synthesising entire text, traps use half-true paragraph summaries

ambiguity_patterns.ambiguity_trigger: >=2 options textually supported at surface level but differ in certainty, scope, or logical necessity

tf_c_rules.TRUE: Explicitly supported or logically necessary byproduct
tf_c_rules.CANNOT_BE_DEDUCED: Not provable OR requires outside assumption

generation_fingerprint:
## RC GENERATION FINGERPRINT

To replicate the Bocconi RC difficulty engine, adhere to the evil answer choices principle: passage complexity is secondary. Cognitive load must be generated entirely through option engineering. The text should be accessible; the path to the correct answer must require rigorous defensive elimination.

OPTION ENGINEERING AND TRAPS:
Never write an obviously false wrong answer. Every distractor must be anchored to the text.
Textual Echo: Lift 3-5 verbatim words from the passage and scramble their syntactic relationship.
Extreme Language: Take a valid passage claim and quietly append an absolute qualifier (completely, only, always, totality).
Outside Knowledge: Write an option representing common sense or real-world fact explicitly missing from the passage.
Scope Shift: Expand a local example into a global rule, or project a present condition into an indefinite future.
Half-True: First clause matches passage exactly, second clause contradicts or overextends.

COGNITIVE LOAD TARGETING:
Literal/Paraphrase: Correct option is semantic mirror of one passage sentence in different vocabulary.
Inference: Require combining >=2 sentences or performing basic mental math.
Deep Inference: Use options >20 words with >=2 clauses. Force >=2 options to survive first-pass elimination.
Answer Similarity Rule: At least 2 distractors MUST survive first-pass elimination, distinguished only by subtle qualifiers.

AMBIGUITY PATTERN:
Embed the primary ambiguity pattern: Plausible vs Supported.
Use weak qualifiers (may, might, suggests) for correct answers.
Use strong qualifiers (must, necessarily, always, only) and scope markers (all, most) to poison otherwise accurate distractors.

ELIMINATION MAPPING RULE (CRITICAL):
Every distractor must correspond to a specific trap type. The generator must be able to state exactly which student mistake leads to selecting each wrong option.
Model: Option B = Textual Echo (student matches keywords without verifying relationship), Option C = Extreme Language (student accepts claim without checking qualifier), Option D = Outside Knowledge (student imports real-world assumption), Option E = Scope Shift (student extends local claim to global rule).
A question is only complete when every wrong answer maps to a traceable cognitive error.
